text: Robotic cholecystectomy with transcystic common bile duct exploration. and Stone's extraction in patient with BMI of seventy one point two. We present a case of a thirty four year old female with a BMI of seventy one point two who suffered from symptomatic cholelithiasis.
The robotic third arm is positioned on the left side of the patient and is actively retracting the liver upwards, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a hook diathermy to dissect the anterior and posterior aspects of Calot's triangle. During the dissection, the cystic duct is visibly dilated.

The surgeon clips and divides a high-rising cystic artery. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging is used to confirm the anatomy of the dilated cystic duct, common bile duct, and their junction, aiding in the identification of suspected stones within the cystic duct.

The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety and proceeds to dissect the gallbladder from the liver guide. The cystic duct is opened, and stones are retrieved using upward stroke pressure applied to the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a Fogarty catheter to remove debris from the cystic duct, successfully extracting an additional stone impacted within the duct.
The surgeon shortens the cystic duct to facilitate the extraction of deeply placed stones, using precise robotic manipulation.

An intraoperative cholangiogram is performed to assess the patency of the common bile duct. The surgeon closes the cystic duct stump using a continuous suture with 4-0 PDS, ensuring secure closure of the duct.
